text.title: Performance Test Types
text.summary: Five test types (load, stress, spike, endurance, scalability) with purpose, duration, and load characteristics.

rule.statement: Always define an SLO (p50, p95, p99 latency, RPS, error rate ceiling) before running any test. Get human approval on the SLO numbers — they are product/business decisions.
rule.rationale: Measuring without a target produces data with no pass/fail verdict. SLO numbers encode business risk tolerance and must not be set unilaterally by an agent.

rule.statement: Establish a baseline on a stable build and commit it to .perf/baseline.json before any optimization work begins.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline, regressions and improvements are undetectable. Baseline must include git SHA, environment, and dataset size as metadata.

section.title: Load Test
section.p: Purpose: verify system under expected steady-state load. Duration: extended (hours). Users: normal to peak expected concurrency. Pass criterion: all SLO thresholds met throughout.

section.title: Stress Test
section.p: Purpose: find the breaking point. Duration: until failure. Users: beyond maximum capacity. Pass criterion: system degrades gracefully (errors, not data corruption).

section.title: Spike Test
section.p: Purpose: test sudden load increases. Duration: short bursts. Users: sudden jump from low to high. Pass criterion: system recovers to baseline latency within N seconds after spike.

section.title: Endurance Test (Soak)
section.p: Purpose: surface memory leaks and resource exhaustion. Duration: 8-72 hours. Users: normal load sustained. Pass criterion: RSS, heap, and FD counts stable throughout.

section.title: Scalability Test
section.p: Purpose: verify horizontal/vertical scaling headroom. Duration: varied. Users: incrementally increasing. Pass criterion: linear throughput scaling with added replicas.

section.title: Tooling Rules

rule.statement: Use coordinated-omission-corrected tools (k6, wrk2, vegeta) for load tests. Never use tools that issue the next request only after the previous response (Apache Bench, naive scripts) — they underreport tail latency.
rule.rationale: Non-corrected tools synchronize request rate to server speed, hiding queuing delay. Real clients do not wait politely.

rule.statement: Run load tests on a staging environment with production-equivalent data volume. Never draw conclusions from local-machine runs or empty-database tests.
rule.rationale: Empty DBs produce optimistic query plans. Local machines have different CPU governor, network, and I/O than production servers.

rule.statement: Warm the cache and JIT for at least 60 seconds before recording results. Discard the ramp-up window from metrics.
rule.rationale: First-run results include import cost, JIT compilation, and cold cache fills. They are not representative of steady-state performance.

section.reference: k6 script with steady + spike scenarios and SLO thresholds
Compare current metrics vs baseline JSON; exit 1 on regression
